title: Sausage and Mash with Caramelised Onions
subtitle: Pan-fried pork sausages with slowly caramelised sweet onions and buttery mash
excerpt: Sausage and mash with caramelised onions is a different dish to bangers and mash with onion gravy. The onions here are cooked for 40 to 45 minutes until completely collapsed and deeply sweet, then piled over the sausages. There is no thickened sauce; the onions are the sauce.
sourceNotes: British home cooking tradition. The combination of pork sausages with caramelised onions is a widely documented British supper dish, distinct from the onion-gravy version by the extended slow cooking of the onions to a jammy, concentrated sweetness.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The caramelised onions can be made up to 3 days ahead and refrigerated. Reheat gently before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: To cook vegetables or sugar slowly until the natural sugars break down and concentrate into a deep golden, sweet paste. With onions, this takes at least 30 to 40 minutes over a low heat and cannot be hurried without burning. The result is quite different from simply softening.

content.text: The caramelised onions take the most time here and do the most work. They need a wide pan, a low heat, and patience: 40 to 45 minutes of slow cooking turns raw, pungent onion into something deeply sweet and complex. Start them before anything else; the sausages and mash can be fitted around them.

items.prepNote: peeled and cut into even chunks

content.text: Melt the butter for the onions in a wide frying pan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} sliced onion and the thyme leaves with a generous pinch of salt. Stir to coat, then reduce the heat to low. Cook, stirring every 5 to 8 minutes, for 40 to 45 minutes until the onions are completely collapsed and a deep, even golden-brown. This is
; it cannot be rushed. Season with salt and pepper when done.
Cook the sausages and make the mash
Heat the oven to 200°C. Place the {{sausages-pork}} sausages in a roasting tin with a little oil and roast for 25 to 30 minutes, turning once, until deeply browned all over.

While the sausages roast, cook the {{potato}} of potato in well-salted boiling water for 18 to 20 minutes until tender. Drain and leave to steam-dry for 2 minutes. Return to the pan over a low heat, mash thoroughly, then beat in the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{whole-milk}} of warm milk until smooth. Season well.
Serve the mash piled with sausages and the caramelised onions spooned generously over the top.

items.symptom: Onions are burning rather than browning
items.cause: Heat too high, or the pan ran dry.
items.fix: Keep the heat at the lowest setting possible. If the onions start to stick and catch, add a tablespoon of water and scrape the base clean. The moisture stops them burning and the resulting steam softens any hardened sugars.

items.symptom: Onions are soft but still pale after 20 minutes
items.cause: Normal. Caramelisation takes longer than most recipes suggest.
items.fix: Keep cooking. The colour change happens slowly and then all at once. Pale-soft onions at 20 minutes are simply not done.

items.cause: Not enough butter and milk, or the potatoes were packed before the fat went in.
items.fix: Add butter and milk generously and work them in while the potato is still very hot. Cold mash does not absorb fat.

content.text: The caramelised onion version of sausage and mash sits in the same place as the gravy version on the British menu, but it is a quieter dish. There is no thickened sauce to hide behind; the sweetness of the onions does all the work. Four onions seems like a lot but they cook down to a small, concentrated pile. Use a good pork sausage with a decent meat content; this combination rewards quality and punishes a pale, watery banger.
